skill.name: pdf
skill.description: PDF manipulation skill for extracting text and tables, creating or modifying documents, merging and splitting files, converting pages to images, and filling PDF forms.
skill.summary: Process, analyze, convert, and fill PDF documents with local Python and command-line tooling.
skill.author: Cam10001110101

security_audit.summary: AI review dismisses the static high-risk C2 and weak-crypto findings as semantic false positives in PDF examples, tests, and documentation. The skill has legitimate medium risk because it provides local scripts that parse arbitrary PDFs and write user-specified output files; no malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection evidence was found.

medium_findings.title: Local PDF Helpers Read and Write User-Specified Files
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The helper scripts intentionally read PDFs, JSON field maps, and images, then write output PDFs, images, or JSON files. This is expected for the skill, but it can overwrite files or process sensitive documents if the user supplies unsafe paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly open caller-provided paths and write derived files. The behavior is legitimate PDF processing, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary PDF Parsing Through Local Libraries
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill routes user-provided PDFs through pypdf, pdf2image, PIL, and related tooling. Parsing complex or untrusted PDFs can expose users to dependency vulnerabilities or resource exhaustion.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The imports and calls show real PDF and image parsing on user-selected files. No exploit or malicious intent is present, but the attack surface is real.

low_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The many external command detections occur in markdown instructions and examples for running local Python scripts or standard PDF tools. No evidence found of hidden subprocess execution, shell injection, or remote command retrieval.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are fenced code blocks or inline command examples. They describe user-invoked local tools instead of programmatic shell execution.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Findings Are Reference Links
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are license terms or documentation references. No evidence found that the skill sends PDF data, secrets, telemetry, or network requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear in comments or legal text only. There are no HTTP client imports or outbound request calls in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and C2 Labels Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static high-risk labels map to form examples, test fixtures, field descriptions, or ordinary PDF file names. No evidence found of cryptographic code, command-and-control behavior, beaconing, persistence, or covert communication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found benign PDF examples and validation logic at the flagged locations. The static labels do not match the code semantics.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE CHECK: A targeted review found no text instructing the evaluator to ignore prior instructions, skip security analysis, or change risk levels. No prompt injection finding is confirmed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed files contain task guidance for PDF processing, not evaluator override language. Confidence is high but not absolute because prompt injection checks are semantic.

content.value_statement: PDF work often requires extracting content, combining files, or completing forms without losing layout. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical workflows for local PDF analysis and editing.
actual_capabilities: Extract text, metadata, and tables from PDF files using Python libraries.
Create, merge, split, rotate, crop, encrypt, decrypt, and watermark PDFs.
Convert PDF pages to images for OCR, visual inspection, and form analysis.
Inspect fillable form fields and write validated field values into PDFs.
Add text annotations to non-fillable forms using measured bounding boxes.
Use local tools such as qpdf, pdftotext, pdfimages, and pdftoppm when available.
limitations: The skill depends on local Python packages and command-line PDF tools being installed.
Scanned PDFs require OCR, and OCR quality depends on image clarity and language support.
PDF forms can vary by viewer, so filled results need visual verification.
The included helper scripts read and write local files chosen by the user.

use_cases.title: Prepare client forms
use_cases.description: Fill structured or scanned PDF forms while validating field positions before final delivery.

use_cases.title: Extract document data
use_cases.description: Pull text, metadata, and tables from PDFs for review, spreadsheets, or downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Automate document assembly
use_cases.description: Merge, split, rotate, crop, watermark, or generate PDFs as part of repeatable workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a PDF
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the text from this PDF, summarize the main sections, and list any pages that need manual review.

prompt_templates.title: Extract tables
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the tables in this PDF, describe their columns, and export the useful rows into a spreadsheet-ready format.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a form
prompt_templates.prompt: Check whether this PDF has fillable fields, map each field to the supplied answers, fill it, and verify the result visually.

prompt_templates.title: Build a PDF workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a repeatable local workflow that splits incoming PDFs, extracts text and tables, applies OCR when needed, and produces final merged PDFs.

output_examples.input: A 30-page report with text, tables, and scanned appendix pages.
output: A section-by-section summary with page references.
A list of extracted tables and any rows needing manual correction.
A note that appendix pages require OCR because they are image-based.

output_examples.input: A PDF application form plus user-provided answers.
output: A completed PDF saved as a new output file.
A validation note for fields that were filled directly.
A visual-review checklist for non-fillable annotation areas.

output_examples.input: Several PDFs that need to become one client packet.
output_examples.output: A merged packet with selected pages, rotated pages corrected, and a final verification summary.

best_practices: Work on copies of important PDFs and keep the original files unchanged.
Visually inspect filled forms before sending or signing them.
Confirm that required local tools and Python packages are installed before starting batch work.
anti_patterns: Do not run helper scripts on untrusted PDFs without normal file safety precautions.
Do not assume OCR output is complete without reviewing low-quality scanned pages.
Do not overwrite the only copy of a document when testing merge, split, or form-fill steps.

faq.question: Can this skill fill PDF forms?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports fillable fields and non-fillable forms that require text annotations.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows are local, although the documentation contains reference URLs.

faq.question: Can it process scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes, when OCR tools are installed. Results should be reviewed for recognition errors.

faq.question: Can it extract tables?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents pdfplumber and related workflows for table extraction.

faq.question: Will formatting always match the original PDF?
faq.answer: No. PDF viewers handle form values and annotations differently, so visual verification is required.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive documents?
faq.answer: It works locally, but users should control file paths, permissions, and retention for sensitive PDFs.
